text: The surgeon performs a right hepatectomy on a fibrotic liver, using laparoscopy to reveal fibrosis and hypertrophic changes in the left lobe. A Pringle maneuver is set up but not used. The right polar vein is clipped and stapled, guided by intraoperative ultrasound to locate the mid-hepatic vein and V8 and V5. A hanging maneuver is employed, with superficial transection using a harmonic device and deeper transection with CUSA and clipping around vascular structures. Portal vein branches are navigated, showing embolic material from prior embolization. The right polar pedicle is retaken with a stapler, and the deep transection continues with CUSA and harmonic devices, clearing the way for stapling V8 and the right hepatic vein.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic right extended hepatectomy in a fibrotic liver. The procedure follows a previous laparoscopic partial ALPS procedure. The right hepatic vein and middle hepatic vein flank the 3.5 cm AGC in segment H. The hilum is approached, isolating and ligating the right portal vein. The surgeon notes hypertrophic changes in the left lobe and inflammatory adhesions. The right hepatic artery is retrieved and dissected back to the hilum. The right hepatic pedicle is stapled, allowing continuation of the deeper parenchymal transection to complete the hepatectomy. The CUSA is used to dissect intraparenchymal structures.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic left lateral sectionectomy in a cirrhotic liver with visible nodularity and ascites. Dense scar tissue surrounding vascular structures is cleared using harmonic, bipolar energy, and CUSA during the parenchymal transection. The transected surface is treated with bipolar energy and suturing to manage bleeding.

In a robotic-assisted partial segment VII hepatectomy on a cirrhotic liver, the surgeon exposes the superficial but posterior lesion by taking down the triangular ligament. Ultrasound guidance reveals a transection plane intersected by a portal pedicle. Parenchymal transection is performed using vessel sealer and bipolar forceps, which also facilitate parenchymal fracture. The dissection around the portal pedicle is less clean, requiring suturing for control. Once the pedicle is controlled proximally, transection is completed with vessel sealer and bipolar forceps, sparing significant parenchyma. The thick cut surface is inspected for reassurance.

The robotic-assisted partial segment V hepatectomy is performed in a cirrhotic liver with significant adhesions from a prior cholecystectomy. The surgeon uses a vessel sealer for transection, encountering bleeding from the portal pedicle, which is managed with compression and a short Pringle maneuver. The robotic approach allows for precise transections around the small lesion in a tight space, although the vascular dissection is less refined compared to the CUSA used in laparoscopic procedures.
